subject: Physics
q: A force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$ acts on a point $\mathrm{B}$ at the end of an L-shaped object, as shown in the figure. The angle $\theta$ that will produce maximum moment of the force about point $\mathrm{A}$ is given by:
question: A force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$ acts on a point $\mathrm{B}$ at the end of an L-shaped object, as shown in the figure. The angle $\theta$ that will produce maximum moment of the force about point $\mathrm{A}$ is given by:
solution:
To produce maximum moment of force line of action of force must be perpendicular to line $\mathrm{AB}$.
$\therefore \tan \theta=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A motor operating on 100 V draws a current of 1 A. If the efficiency of the motor is $91.6 \%$, then the loss of power in units of $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}$ is
question: A motor operating on 100 V draws a current of 1 A. If the efficiency of the motor is $91.6 \%$, then the loss of power in units of $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {input }}=V i=100 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \eta=\frac{P_{\text {out }}}{P_{\text {input }}}=0.916\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {out }}=91.6 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \text { Loss }=100-91.6=8.4 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : Planck's constant and angular momentum have the same dimensions.

Statement (II) : Linear momentum and moment of force have the same dimensions.

In light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : Planck's constant and angular momentum have the same dimensions.

Statement (II) : Linear momentum and moment of force have the same dimensions.

In light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
The dimensions of the required quantities can be written as

E=hν⇒h=Eν⇒[h]=ML2T-2T-1=ML2T-1

[L]=mvr=MLT-1L=ML2 T-1

[p]=mv=MLT-1=ML1 T-1

[τ]=Fr=MLT-2L=ML2 T-2

where, h  is Planck's constant, L is angular momentum, p is linear momentum and τ is moment of force.

From the above analysis, it can be concluded that Statement I is true, but statement II is false.

q: A plano-convex lens of refractive index <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and focal length <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is kept in contact with another plano-concave lens of refractive index <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and focal length <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of curvature of their spherical faces is <math><mi>R</mi></math> each and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> the <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are related as:
question: A plano-convex lens of refractive index <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and focal length <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is kept in contact with another plano-concave lens of refractive index <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and focal length <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of curvature of their spherical faces is <math><mi>R</mi></math> each and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> the <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are related as:
solution:
Given,

f1=2f2

Lens maker formula for the first lens is given by,

1f1=μ1-11R-1∞      ...(1)

Lens maker formula for the second lens is given by,

1f2=μ2-11R-1∞      ...(2)

After 12, we get:

f2f1=μ1-1μ2-1=12

⇒2μ1-2=μ2-1

⇒2μ1-μ2=1

subject: Physics
q: A parallel beam of light travelling in air (refractive index 1.0) is incident on a convex spherical glass surface of radius of curvature 50 cm. Refractive index of glass is 1.5. The rays converge to a point at a distance $x \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the curvature of the spherical surface. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: A parallel beam of light travelling in air (refractive index 1.0) is incident on a convex spherical glass surface of radius of curvature 50 cm. Refractive index of glass is 1.5. The rays converge to a point at a distance $x \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the curvature of the spherical surface. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For refraction at a single spherical surface, we use the formula: $\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R}$
Given data:
Refractive index of air $\mu_1 = 1.0$
Refractive index of glass $\mu_2 = 1.5$
Radius of curvature $R = +50$ cm (convex surface)
Object is at infinity for a parallel beam, so $u = -\infty$
Substituting the values:
$\frac{1.5}{v} - \frac{1.0}{-\infty} = \frac{1.5 - 1.0}{50}$
$\frac{1.5}{v} - 0 = \frac{0.5}{50}$
$\frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{1}{100}$
$v = 1.5 \times 100 = 150$ cm
The image is formed at a distance of $150$ cm from the pole (vertex) of the spherical surface.
The question asks for the distance $x$ from the centre of curvature.
Distance from centre of curvature $x = |v - R|$
$x = 150 - 50 = 100$ cm

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
question:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
options: Impulse and average force acting on the object will be same for both the cases.
Impulse will be same for both the cases but the average force will be different.
Average force will be same for both the cases but the impulse will be different.
Average force and impulse will be different for both the cases.
solution:
Impulse = Change in momentum

I=ΔP

Therefore, in both the cases the impulse is same.

Average force is defined as,

Favg =ΔPΔt

Since the time in the two cases is different, the average force will be different.

subject: Physics
q: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
question: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
From A→B, using the equation of motion, we have

-80=-v1t-12×10t2⇒-80=-2v1-12×10×22⇒-80=-2v1-20⇒-60=-2v1⇒v1=30 m s-1

From O to A, it follows that

v2=u2+2gS⇒302=0+2×(-10)(-S)⇒900=20 S⇒S=45 m

question: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
solution:
Bandwidth =2×fm

=2×105 Hz=200 kHz

subject: Physics
q: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
question: Consider a gas of triatomic molecules. The molecules  are assumed to be triangular and made of massless rigid rods whose vertices are occupied by atoms. The internal energy of a mole of the gas at temperature T is:
solution:
The formula of internal energy of a gas is given by

U=f2RT

Here R is the gas constant and T is temperature.

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1

For the given configuration of triatomic molecules, the degree of freedom f=6

⇒ U=62RT=3RT

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
solution:
Given: Amplitude of electric field,
$$
E_0=4 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{m}
$$
Absolute permitivity,
$$
\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{c}^2 / \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}^2
$$
Average energy density $u_E=$ ?
Applying formula,
Average energy density $u_E=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_0 E^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \quad u_E=\frac{1}{4} \times 8.8 \times 10^{-12} \times(4)^2 \\
&=35.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

The weight of that person if the lift descends with uniform downward acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be <u>              </u> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A person standing on a spring balance inside a stationary lift measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The weight of that person if the lift descends with uniform downward acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be <u>              </u> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
When lift is at rest

N=mg

⇒60×10=600 N

When lift moves with downward acceleration. In frame of lift pseudo force will be in upward direction.

N'=Mg-a

⇒6010-1.8

N'⇒492 N

subject: Physics
q: A potentiometer wire of length 300 cm is connected in series with a resistance 780 Ω and a standard cell of emf 4 V. A constant current flows through potentiometer wire. The length of the null point for cell of emf 20mV is found to be 60 cm. The resistance of the potentiometer wire is _____ Ω.
question: A potentiometer wire of length 300 cm is connected in series with a resistance 780 Ω and a standard cell of emf 4 V. A constant current flows through potentiometer wire. The length of the null point for cell of emf 20mV is found to be 60 cm. The resistance of the potentiometer wire is _____ Ω.
solution:
Let resistance of potentiometers wire is R, then the current through AB

iAB=4R+780

Potential difference across AB

VAB=4RR+780

Potential difference across AC

VAC=VABlL=4RR+780×60300=4R5R+780

This should be equal to 20 mV

⇒4R5R+780=20×10-3=2×10-2

⇒4R=10-1R+780

⇒4R-R10=78

⇒39R10=78

⇒R=20 Ω

q: An engine takes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> moles of air at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>, and compresses it adiabatically to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of the original volume. Assuming air to be a diatomic ideal gas made up of rigid molecules, the change in its internal energy during this process comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XkJ</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is:
question: An engine takes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> moles of air at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>, and compresses it adiabatically to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of the original volume. Assuming air to be a diatomic ideal gas made up of rigid molecules, the change in its internal energy during this process comes out to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>XkJ</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is:

=T1(10)75-1

ΔV=52nR;52×5×3102/5-1(293)

6256×1.5×293=461440

≈46ks

subject: Physics
q: A small square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The loops are coplanar and their centers coincide. The value of the mutual inductance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A small square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The loops are coplanar and their centers coincide. The value of the mutual inductance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
Flux linkage for inner loop

ϕ=Bcenter×l2

=4×μ0i4πL2sin45°+sin45°l2

⇒ϕ=22μ0iπLl2

Therefore, mutual inductance will be

M=ϕi=22μ0l2πL=22μ0π

=224ππ×10-7

=82×10-7

=128×10-7 H

Hence, x=128.

subject: Physics
q: A dc source of emf $\mathrm{E}_1=100 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r=0.5 \Omega$, a storage battery of emf $\mathrm{E}_2=90 \mathrm{~V}$ and an external resistance $\mathrm{R}$ are connected as shown in figure. For what value of R no current will pass through the battery?
question: A dc source of emf $\mathrm{E}_1=100 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r=0.5 \Omega$, a storage battery of emf $\mathrm{E}_2=90 \mathrm{~V}$ and an external resistance $\mathrm{R}$ are connected as shown in figure. For what value of R no current will pass through the battery?
solution:
$\frac{100}{R+r}=\frac{90}{R}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{10}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{10}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{9} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{R}=4.5 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

u=0;  initially at rest
v= at
C. X=12at2 (particle was initially at origin) v→t graph will be parabola passing through origin. Option D is correct.

q: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
question: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
solution:
Carrier wave frequency =250×10=2500 kHz

Frequency used 2500±250k Ω=2250 kHz to 2750 kHz

The allotted frequency showed be such that f±250 kHz should not overlap with previous one. Hence possible frequency =2000 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass 10 g moves in a straight line with retardation 2x, where x is the displacement in SI units. Its loss of kinetic energy for above displacement is 10x-nJ. The value of n will be ________.
question: A particle of mass 10 g moves in a straight line with retardation 2x, where x is the displacement in SI units. Its loss of kinetic energy for above displacement is 10x-nJ. The value of n will be ________.
solution:
Acceleration can be written as, a=vdvdx.

Given: a=-2x(-ve sign is due to retardation)

Therefore,

vdvdx=-2x⇒vdv=-2xdx

Integrating both sides, we get

12vf2-vi2=-2x22=x2⇒12mvf2-vi2=mx2

⇒|ΔKE|=0.01x2=10x-2

Hence, n=2.

q: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> times the energy required to remove an electron from helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is close to
question: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> times the energy required to remove an electron from helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is close to
solution: The energy is required to remove <math> <mrow> <msup> <mtext>e</mtext> <mo>−</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> from singly ionized helium atom<math><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>54.4</mn><mi> </mi><mn>e</mn><mn>V</mn></math>. The energy required to remove <math> <mrow> <msup> <mtext>e</mtext> <mo>−</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> from a helium atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mi> eV</mi></math>, given <math><mn>54.4 </mn><mn>e</mn><mn>V</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>2.2 </mn><mi>x</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>24.73</mn><mi> </mi><mn>e</mn><mn>V</mn></math>. The energy required to ionize helium atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>79.13</mn><mi> </mi><mn>e</mn><mn>V</mn><mo>≈</mo><mn>79</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>.

subject: Physics
q: In an adiabatic process, the density of a diatomic gas becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times its initial value. The final pressure of the gas is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the initial pressure. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is:
question: In an adiabatic process, the density of a diatomic gas becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times its initial value. The final pressure of the gas is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the initial pressure. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given,

Final density of diatomic gas is ρf=32nρi

Final pressure is Pf=nPi

In adiabatic process, equation of state is given by,

PVγ=constant  ...1

Where, V is the volume, P is the pressure and γ=75 is the ratio of specific heat of diatomic gas

Pmργ=constant  ...2, As V=mρ

and mass is constant, then the pressure is given by

∴P∝ργ

⇒PfPi=ρfρiγ

⇒nPiPi=32ρiρi7/5=(32)7/5

∴ n=27=128

subject: Physics
q: Two balls of same mass and carrying equal charge are hung from a fixed support of length $l$. At electrostatic equilibrium, assuming that angles made by each thread is small, the separation, $x$ between the balls is proportional to :
question: Two balls of same mass and carrying equal charge are hung from a fixed support of length $l$. At electrostatic equilibrium, assuming that angles made by each thread is small, the separation, $x$ between the balls is proportional to :
solution: In equilibrium, $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{T} \sin \theta$ $m g=T \cos \theta$ $\tan \theta=\frac{F_e}{m g}=\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^2 \times m g}$ also $\tan \theta \approx \sin =\frac{x / 2}{\ell}$ Hence, $\frac{x}{2 \ell}=\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^2 \times m g}$ $\Rightarrow x^3=\frac{2 q^2 \ell}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 m g}$ $\therefore x=\left(\frac{q^2 \ell}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 m g}\right)^{1 / 3}$ Therefore $\mathrm{x} \propto \ell^{1 / 3}$

subject: Physics
q: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
solution:
In case of SHM in spring-block system, angular frequency is given by, ω=km.

Therefore,

ω2ω1=m1m2=12

subject: Physics
q: A tuning fork vibrates with frequency $256 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and gives one best per second with the third normal mode of vibration of an open pipe. What is the length of the pipe? (Speed of sound of air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
question: A tuning fork vibrates with frequency $256 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and gives one best per second with the third normal mode of vibration of an open pipe. What is the length of the pipe? (Speed of sound of air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
solution:
According to question, tuning fork gives 1 beat/second with (N) $3^{\text {rd }}$ normal mode. Therefore, organ pipe will have frequency $(256 \pm 1) \mathrm{Hz}$. In open organ pipe, frequency $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{NV}}{2 \ell}$
or, $255=\frac{3 \times 340}{2 \times \ell} \Rightarrow \ell=2 \mathrm{~m}=200 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
question:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
solution:
Linear antennas are considered to be those antennas that imply the use of electrically thin conductors having diameter much less than the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave passing through them.

The formula to calculate the effective power radiated by a linear antenna is given by

Peff=kl2λ2

where, k is a constant.

subject: Physics
q: Two parallel plate capacitors of capacity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are connected in parallel combination and charged to a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The battery is then disconnected and the space between the plates of the capacitor of capacity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is completely filled with a material of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math>. The final potential difference across the combination of capacitors will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: Two parallel plate capacitors of capacity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are connected in parallel combination and charged to a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The battery is then disconnected and the space between the plates of the capacitor of capacity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is completely filled with a material of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math>. The final potential difference across the combination of capacitors will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
The charge on the capacitor is given by q=CV.

Initial charge on C=18CV

And initial charge on 3C=54CV

Let final common potential difference =V'

When a dielectric of dielectric constant k is introduced between the plates of the capacitor the capacitance becomes k times.

Applying conservation of charge,

9CV'+3CV'=18CV+54CV

⇒12CV'=72CV⇒V'=6 V
